step.title: Step 26
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 8 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 164, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: CRITICAL - SPACE switch caused target (48,33) to reset from W to b. Collections do NOT persist across entity switches. Entities moved: B@45,33, p@63,20, w@63,52. Cursor now at (42,48) on a different entity.
- Goal model: Need to understand collection mechanic better. Targets only stay collected while entity is on them.
- Plan: Test collecting an orange target with current entity from (42,48). Target O@[42,24] is LEFT 8 away.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 143 actions; a strong score needs about 42 or fewer. You are 3.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The target at (42, 24) is MISSING from the list now. Before it was O@[42, 24], but now it's gone. This means the target WAS collected (it disappeared, not just turned white).
Also, the entities moved again:
- B: center=(47, 33), hash=555b7b45 (was 45, 33)
- p: center=(63, 18), hash=5f83bfa7 (was 63, 20)
- w: center=(63, 50), hash=cdce2941 (was 63, 52)
The entities are drifting/moving even when I'm not controlling them. This is important.
Current state:
- Cursor at (42, 24)
- Target O@[42, 24] collected (disappeared)
- Remaining targets: 3 red, 3 blue, 2 orange (8 total)
  - r@[3, 21], r@[9, 27], r@[12, 12]
  - b@[36, 27], b@[48, 15], b@[48, 33], b@[60, 27]
  - O@[39, 9], O@[57, 9]
Wait, I count 3+4+2 = 9 targets, not 8. Let me recount:
- r@[3, 21], r@[9, 27], r@[12, 12] - 3 red
- b@[36, 27], b@[48, 15], b@[48, 33], b@[60, 27] - 4 blue
- O@[39, 9], O@[57, 9] - 2 orange
Total: 9 targets remaining (was 10, collected 1)
So the collection DOES work - targets disappear when collected. The earlier observation that targets "reset" was because I was looking at the wrong thing. When I moved the blue cross away from (48, 33), the target at (48, 33) was still there (blue), but when I collected the orange target at (42, 24), it disappeared.
Wait, but earlier when I collected (36, 27) with the blue cross, it turned WHITE, not disappeared. And when I collected (60, 27), it also turned WHITE. But now (42, 24) disappeared completely.
Let me re-examine. The targets are detected as children of the 'c' (charcoal) parent object. When a target is collected, it might:
1. Turn WHITE (W) - as seen with blue targets
2. Disappear completely - as seen with orange target (42, 24)
This is confusing. Let me check if (42, 24) is really gone or if it's just not being detected as a child of 'c'.
Actually, looking at the output, the targets are listed as children of 'c' objects with 8 pixels. The orange target at (42, 24) was O@[42, 24], and now it's not in the list. So it was collected.
But the blue targets at (36, 27), (48, 15), (48, 33), (60, 27) are still there. When I was on (48, 33) earlier, it turned WHITE but didn't disappear. This suggests:
- Blue targets turn WHITE when collected (but don't disappear)
- Orange targets disappear when collected
- Or there's some other difference
Actually, I think the issue is that when I collected (48, 33) with the blue cross, it turned WHITE. But then when I switched entities with SPACE, the target reset to blue. This suggests the collection was temporary.
But when I collected (42, 24) with the current entity (which one is it?), the target disappeared completely. This suggests the collection was permanent.
The difference might be:
- Blue cross collecting blue targets: temporary (turns WHITE, resets when entity moves away)
- Current entity collecting orange targets: permanent (disappears)
Or maybe the current entity is not the blue cross. Let me check which entity I'm controlling.
The cursor is at (42, 24), and the entities are at B@47,33, p@63,18, w@63,50. The cursor is not on any of these entity centers. So the cursor is independent of the entities.
Wait, I think I've been misunderstanding the game. The cursor is the player's position, and the entities are obstacles or other objects. The player collects targets by moving the cursor onto them.
But earlier, when I moved the cursor to (36, 27), the blue target turned WHITE. And when I moved to (60, 27), the blue target turned WHITE. And now when I moved to (42, 24), the orange target disappeared.
The difference might be the entity color. The cursor color might change based on which entity it's "attached" to. When I pressed SPACE, the cursor switched to a different entity, and now it can collect orange targets permanently.
Let me check the cursor color. The cursor is a white pixel (W), but maybe it has an associated entity color.
Actually, I think the key insight is:
- The cursor can collect targets of a specific color based on the current entity
- When the cursor is on the blue cross entity, it collects blue targets (turns them WHITE)
- When the cursor is on the orange X entity, it collects orange targets (makes them disappear)
- When the cursor is on the red diamond entity, it collects red targets
And the collection is permanent - targets don't reset. The earlier observation that (48, 33) reset was because I switched entities before the collection was "locked in".
Let me test this by collecting more orange targets with the current entity.
From (42, 24), the remaining orange targets are:
- O@[39, 9]: UP 1, LEFT 5
- O@[57, 9]: DOWN 5, LEFT 5
Let me collect O@[39, 9] first.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: KEY INSIGHT - Target O@[42,24] DISAPPEARED (collected permanently). This is different from blue targets which turned WHITE temporarily. The current entity (after SPACE switch) can permanently collect orange targets. Entities are drifting: B@47,33, p@63,18, w@63,50.
Goal model: Collect all targets permanently. Each entity type collects its matching color permanently.
Plan: Collect remaining orange targets with current entity: O@[39,9] and O@[57,9]. Then switch to collect blue and red targets.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current cursor: (42, 24)
# Remaining orange targets: O@[39, 9], O@[57, 9]
# From (42, 24) to (39, 9): UP 1, LEFT 5

action(['UP'] + ['LEFT']*5)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] == 1:
        print(f"Cursor: {n['boundary'][0]}")

print("Targets:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 8:
        for cid in n['children']:
            cn = nodes[cid]
            print(f"  {cn['color']}@{cn['boundary'][0]}")

print(f"Game over: {last_action_result.get('game_over', False)}")

# Now collect O@[57, 9]
# From (39, 9) to (57, 9): DOWN 6
action(['DOWN']*6)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] == 1:
        print(f"\nCursor: {n['boundary'][0]}")

print("Targets after collecting O@[57,9]:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 8:
        for cid in n['children']:
            cn = nodes[cid]
            print(f"  {cn['color']}@{cn['boundary'][0]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
